subject: Science
chapter: The World of Metals and Non-metals

key_points: Materials are broadly classified into metals and non-metals based on their physical and chemical properties.
Metals are generally hard, lustrous (shiny), and good conductors of heat and electricity.
Non-metals can be solids, liquids, or gases and are generally poor conductors.
Malleability is the property of metals to be beaten into thin sheets.
Ductility is the property of metals by which they can be drawn into wires.
Metals produce a ringing sound when struck, a property known as sonority.
examples: Metals: Iron (Fe), Copper (Cu), Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Aluminum (Al)
Non-metals: Oxygen (O), Hydrogen (H), Carbon (C), Sulphur (S), Iodine (I)
Lustrous Non-metal: Iodine (an exception as most non-metals are dull)
Liquid Metal: Mercury (found in thermometers, liquid at room temperature)
Soft Metals: Sodium and Potassium (can be cut with a knife)
formulas: Metal + Oxygen → Metal Oxide (Basic in nature)
Non-metal + Oxygen → Non-metal Oxide (Acidic in nature)
Rusting of Iron: $4Fe + 3O_2 + 6H_2O ightarrow 4Fe(OH)_3$ (Hydrated Iron Oxide)
exam_tips: Remember that Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.
Use the 'Sonorous' property to explain why bells are made of metal and not wood.
Note that Graphite (a form of Carbon) is a non-metal that conducts electricity.
